examples.title: Algorithm: System Call Invocation Mechanism
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how a user program invokes a system call and transitions from user mode to kernel mode
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the complete flow of a system call: the user program places the system call number and parameters in registers, triggers a TRAP (software interrupt), the CPU switches to kernel mode, the kernel validates and dispatches to the appropriate handler via the system call table, executes the operation, and returns control to user mode with the result.

examples.title: Python: Using System Calls via os and sys Modules
examples.description: Demonstrates how Python programs use system calls through the os and sys modules to perform file I/O and process operations
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Python program demonstrating system calls usage through the os and sys modules.
These modules provide Python wrappers around the underlying OS system calls.
"""

import os
import sys

def demonstrate_file_system_calls():
 """Demonstrates file-related system calls: open, read, write, close"""
 
 print("=== File System System Calls Demo ===\n")
 
 # System call: open() - returns file descriptor (integer)
 # In Linux: maps to sys_open system call
 filename = "demo_file.txt"
 fd = os.open(filename, os.O_CREAT | os.O_WRONLY | os.O_TRUNC, 0o644)
 print(f"[System Call] open() returned file descriptor: {fd}")
 
 # System call: write() - writes data to file
 # In Linux: maps to sys_write system call
 message = b"Hello from Python using system calls!\n"
 bytes_written = os.write(fd, message)
 print(f"[System Call] write() wrote {bytes_written} bytes")
 
 # System call: close() - closes the file descriptor
 # In Linux: maps to sys_close system call
 os.close(fd)
 print(f"[System Call] close() closed file descriptor {fd}")
 
 # Reopen to read: system call open() again
 fd_read = os.open(filename, os.O_RDONLY)
 print(f"[System Call] open() for reading, fd: {fd_read}")
 
 # System call: read() - reads data from file
 # In Linux: maps to sys_read system call
 buffer = os.read(fd_read, 1024)
 print(f"[System Call] read() read: {buffer.decode()}")
 
 os.close(fd_read)
 print(f"[System Call] close() closed file descriptor {fd_read}")
 
 # System call: unlink() - deletes file (Linux: sys_unlink)
 os.unlink(filename)
 print(f"[System Call] unlink() deleted file: {filename}")

def demonstrate_process_system_calls():
 """Demonstrates process-related system calls: fork, getpid, getuid"""
 
 print("\n=== Process Management System Calls Demo ===\n")
 
 # System call: getpid() - get process ID
 # In Linux: maps to sys_getpid system call
 pid = os.getpid()
 print(f"[System Call] getpid() returned: {pid}")
 
 # System call: getuid() - get user ID
 # In Linux: maps to sys_getuid system call
 uid = os.getuid()
 print(f"[System Call] getuid() returned: {uid}")
 
 # System call: fork() - create new process
 # In Linux: maps to sys_fork system call
 child_pid = os.fork()
 
 if child_pid == 0:
 # This is the child process
 print(f"[Child] My PID: {os.getpid()}, Parent PID: {os.getppid()}")
 os._exit(0) # System call: _exit()
 else:
 # This is the parent process
 print(f"[Parent] Forked child with PID: {child_pid}")
 # System call: wait() - wait for child process
 os.waitpid(child_pid, 0)
 print(f"[Parent] Child process finished")

def demonstrate_directory_system_calls():
 """Demonstrates directory-related system calls"""
 
 print("\n=== Directory System Calls Demo ===\n")
 
 # System call: getcwd() - get current working directory
 # In Linux: maps to sys_getcwd system call
 cwd = os.getcwd()
 print(f"[System Call] getcwd() returned: {cwd}")
 
 # System call: mkdir() - create directory
 # In Linux: maps to sys_mkdir system call
 test_dir = "test_syscall_dir"
 os.mkdir(test_dir)
 print(f"[System Call] mkdir() created directory: {test_dir}")
 
 # System call: rmdir() - remove directory
 # In Linux: maps to sys_rmdir system call
 os.rmdir(test_dir)
 print(f"[System Call] rmdir() removed directory: {test_dir}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
 print("Python System Calls Demonstration")
 print("=" * 50)
 
 demonstrate_file_system_calls()
 demonstrate_process_system_calls()
 demonstrate_directory_system_calls()
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 50)
 print("All system calls executed successfully!")
examples.explanation: This Python program demonstrates how application programs use system calls through Python's os and sys modules. Each function call in Python (like os.open, os.read, os.write, os.fork) ultimately invokes the corresponding system call in the OS kernel. The program shows file I/O operations, process management (fork/wait), and directory operations, with comments indicating which system call each Python function maps to.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for read/write operations where n is bytes transferred
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for buffer allocation where n is buffer size

examples.title: C: Direct System Calls for File and Process Operations
examples.description: Demonstrates direct system calls in C using the POSIX API to perform file I/O and process management, showing the actual interface between user programs and the kernel
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates direct system calls in UNIX/Linux. The functions like open(), read(), write(), close(), fork(), getpid(), getuid(), mkdir(), rmdir() are POSIX API wrapper functions that internally invoke the corresponding kernel system calls (sys_open, sys_read, sys_write, etc.). The program shows file I/O operations using file descriptors, process creation with fork(), and directory operations. Each section includes comments indicating the mapping between the C function call and the underlying kernel system call.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for read/write where n is bytes transferred; O(1) for open/close/getpid
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for buffer allocation in read operations
